slides.title: You Can Throw Errors Too
slides.body: Python raises errors when *it* detects a problem — but you can raise them yourself. This is how you **fail loudly and early**, catching bad input the moment it arrives.
slides.tip: Better to crash with a clear message now than to silently produce wrong results later.

slides.title: raise Stops Everything, Right Now
slides.body: Use `raise ExceptionType("your message")` to signal a problem instantly. The function stops and the error travels up to whoever called it.
slides.tip: Pick the right built-in: ValueError for bad values, TypeError for wrong types.

slides.title: Name Your Own Exception Types
slides.body: When built-in types are too generic, create a **custom exception** by subclassing `Exception`. One line of `pass` is all you need to get started.
slides.tip: Always end custom exception names in Error — e.g. InsufficientFundsError, not InsufficientFunds.

slides.title: Catch Custom Exceptions by Name
slides.body: Callers can `except` your custom exception by its exact class name, making error-handling code **self-documenting** and precise.
slides.tip: A bare raise inside except re-raises the same exception — handy for logging before propagating.

slides.title: You're Writing Reliable Code Now
slides.body: You can **raise** built-in exceptions, create **custom exception classes**, and re-raise errors after logging. Your programs now fail loudly with clear messages instead of silently doing the wrong thing.
slides.tip: Never use except: pass — it hides bugs and makes code nearly impossible to debug.
